question: Centralization in organizations means that:
options: All employees work in the same physical location

Decision-making authority is concentrated at higher levels of the organizational hierarchy — top management retains most decision-making power and lower-level employees have limited authority to make decisions on their own
The company's data and IT systems are hosted in a single data center
All departments report to the same manager regardless of function
explanation: Centralization spectrum: fully centralized = all decisions made by top management; fully decentralized = all decisions made by frontline employees. Most organizations are somewhere in between. Highly centralized examples: McDonald's global brand standards (no individual franchisee can change the Big Mac recipe); Catholic Church (doctrinal decisions made centrally by Vatican). Highly decentralized examples: Berkshire Hathaway (Warren Buffett explicitly gives subsidiary CEOs full operational autonomy); Johnson & Johnson (each of 250+ operating companies makes most decisions independently). The tradeoff: centralization ensures consistency and control; decentralization enables speed and local adaptation.

question: Which of the following situations favors a MORE CENTRALIZED decision-making structure?
options: Highly diverse markets where local conditions vary significantly

Decisions requiring consistency across the organization (brand standards, financial policies, safety standards, legal compliance) — where deviation would be harmful; when company-wide expertise is concentrated in central functions; when economies of scale in decision-making are significant (e.g., one procurement team negotiating for all divisions gets better prices than each division negotiating independently); when decisions carry high risk and require senior oversight
Fast-moving markets where quick response to local conditions is critical
Employee development goals requiring decision-making experience
explanation: Centralization fits: standardized processes (safety procedures cannot be customized by local managers), high-stakes decisions (major capital investments), legal and compliance decisions (one legal team provides consistency), and knowledge concentration (specialized expertise better deployed from a center than replicated everywhere). The McDonald's example: food safety standards are centralized because a local outbreak anywhere damages the global brand. Pricing strategy is centralized because a manager offering local discounts could create arbitrage across markets. However, hiring local employees is decentralized to local managers who know local labor markets.

question: Berkshire Hathaway's organizational philosophy (Warren Buffett) gives almost total operational autonomy to subsidiary CEOs. The GEICO CEO, See's Candies CEO, and Burlington Northern CEO each run their companies with minimal oversight from Buffett. What does this extreme decentralization require to work?
options: Extreme decentralization requires Buffett to personally approve all major decisions

Decentralization requires: (1) Excellent manager selection — Buffett invests enormous time in identifying CEOs he trusts with autonomy; 'I hire people who know more about their business than I do' is his stated approach; (2) Strong capital allocation discipline at the center — Buffett doesn't tell GEICO how to sell insurance, but Berkshire decides capital allocation between subsidiaries (which business gets reinvestment capital); (3) Aligned incentives — subsidiary CEOs whose compensation is tied to their subsidiary's long-term performance have aligned interests; (4) No interference culture — Buffett explicitly will not override subsidiary operating decisions, only capital allocation; (5) Capability fit — the model only works if the subsidiaries truly have managers capable of running their businesses without corporate oversight
Decentralization requires more corporate oversight staff to monitor each subsidiary
Berkshire's model works because all subsidiaries are in the same industry
explanation: Berkshire's decentralization prerequisites: the model works because of extraordinary manager selection + capital allocation control. Buffett's two non-negotiables: (1) Don't compromise the reputation of Berkshire in any way; (2) Report problems to Berkshire immediately, not after you've tried to fix them. Beyond these, operational autonomy is complete. The reason for the model: 'It's hard for me to micromanage 75 businesses, and even harder for them to run well with my micromanaging.' The centralized capital allocation function (Berkshire's 'float' from insurance operations provides perpetual capital that Buffett allocates to highest-return uses across the portfolio) is where Buffett genuinely adds value — not in running insurance or railroad operations.

question: A global consumer goods company's marketing team debates: should pricing be set globally by headquarters or locally by country managers? Which decision-making approach is most appropriate and why?
options: Global pricing is always superior since it ensures brand consistency

It depends on product type and market structure: arguments for central pricing: brand consistency (luxury goods: Louis Vuitton prices globally near-uniformly to prevent arbitrage and maintain prestige perception); global cost benchmarking (prevents unnecessary price variation from local negotiation weakness); protection against gray market arbitrage (if significant price gaps exist between markets, goods flow from low-price to high-price markets through informal channels). Arguments for local pricing: different income levels across markets require different price points (a fixed $10 price works in the US but may be unaffordable in emerging markets); competitive dynamics vary by market (dominant local competitor may require different pricing response); distribution costs vary by market; the optimal solution for most global consumer goods companies: global pricing guardrails (minimum and maximum relative to a global index) with local execution authority within the guardrails
Local pricing is always superior since country managers know their markets best
Pricing decisions should never be delegated below VP level in any organization
explanation: Pricing centralization in global companies: the gray market arbitrage problem is real. If Apple iPhones cost $800 in the US and $700 in a lower-income market, entrepreneurs will buy in the cheaper market and resell in the US — undermining the pricing architecture. Apple maintains global near-parity pricing partly to prevent this. But Unilever's emerging market brands (Surf detergent in India, sold in small 'sachets' at 5 cents vs. the $8 box format in developed markets) require local pricing adaptation to serve different income levels. The centralized guardrails model: headquarters sets price points relative to a global index and category rules; local markets can adjust within defined ranges based on competitive and income dynamics.

question: A major bank has decentralized its lending decisions: branch managers can approve loans up to $500K independently. Loan losses increase significantly. An investigation reveals: branch managers are approving risky loans to meet their loan volume targets. What does this represent about the limits of decentralization?
options: Decentralization is always problematic and banks should centralize all decisions

Decentralization without aligned incentives and controls creates misaligned behavior: the delegation of authority ($500K approval) was designed to speed loan approvals; but the incentive structure (branch managers rewarded for loan volume) created misalignment; the branch manager rational calculation: 'I get credit for approving loans quickly; I don't personally bear the credit risk; therefore I should approve loans aggressively to hit my volume target'; this is the principal-agent problem — the delegatee (branch manager) acts in their own interest (volume bonus) rather than the principal's interest (bank's long-term profitability); the fix: align incentives (reward risk-adjusted return on loans, not volume); strengthen the control system (central credit review for loans above $200K, statistical monitoring of branch loan performance vs. risk ratings)
Volume-based incentives are always the correct metric for bank branch managers
Centralized approval eliminates all risk of misaligned lending decisions
explanation: Principal-agent problem in decentralization: delegating authority without aligning incentives is one of the most common organizational failures. The 2008 financial crisis had multiple examples of this: mortgage originators (branch managers equivalent) were compensated on loan volume with no risk retention → approved sub-prime mortgages they would never have approved if they had to hold the credit risk. The 2008 Countrywide Financial case: 'approve the loan, sell it to investors, collect your fee, repeat' — no incentive to care about loan quality. The lesson: decentralization decisions must simultaneously design: (1) The authority delegation (what can the agent decide?); (2) The incentive structure (what is the agent rewarded for?); (3) The control system (what oversight ensures the agent doesn't abuse the authority?). These three elements must be designed together.

question: Toyota's Quality Circles — where frontline production workers meet weekly to identify and solve quality problems in their own work area — represent which aspect of decentralization?
options: Quality Circles are a form of centralized quality control since they report to management

Operational problem-solving decentralized to the workers with the most direct knowledge: Toyota's insight is that frontline workers know the details of their own processes better than managers or engineers who design from above; quality problems in a specific assembly step are best identified and solved by the people doing that step daily; Quality Circles: (1) Empower workers to make improvements within their own work area; (2) Leverage local knowledge that management doesn't have; (3) Build worker engagement and ownership; (4) Create continuous improvement culture that doesn't require management initiation for every improvement; the organizational design principle: decentralize problem-solving to the level with the most relevant knowledge; centralize decisions requiring system-wide coordination or significant capital
Toyota's Quality Circles are a marketing initiative rather than an operational practice
Frontline workers should never be given authority to change processes
explanation: Toyota Quality Circles and TPS: the Kaizen (continuous improvement) philosophy requires that improvement be everyone's job, not just management's. Quality Circles: groups of 5–8 workers in the same work area meet weekly for 30–60 minutes to: identify problems they've observed, analyze root causes using simple tools (5-whys, fishbone diagram), develop and test solutions, and implement improvements in their area. Toyota tracks number of improvement suggestions per employee/year — world-class Toyota factories generate 10–15 suggestions/employee/year vs. 1–2/employee/year in average organizations. Each suggestion is evaluated and ~80% are implemented. This decentralized improvement engine generates thousands of incremental improvements annually that collectively produce major productivity and quality gains.

question: A company's IT security policy requires that all data be stored on company-approved servers; no cloud services can be used without IT approval. A marketing team, frustrated by slow IT approval processes, starts using Dropbox and Google Docs for project collaboration without approval. What does this 'shadow IT' phenomenon reveal about centralization?
options: Shadow IT is entirely the marketing team's fault and should be addressed through disciplinary action

Shadow IT reveals when centralized control creates friction that exceeds the perceived benefit: the IT approval process (centralized control mechanism) was designed to manage security risk; but when the process takes weeks and blocks productive work, users find workarounds that achieve their goals while technically violating policy; the outcome is worse than either extreme: (1) Marketing uses unapproved cloud services (security risk — the stated concern of centralized control); (2) IT has lost visibility into what services are being used (unknown risk is worse than known risk); the root cause: the centralization benefit (security control) was not designed with sufficient attention to the cost (usability friction that drives workarounds); the resolution: either streamline the approval process (faster, lighter-weight), pre-approve low-risk services, or accept controlled decentralization (IT sets security standards, teams select from approved service catalog)
Centralized IT control always prevents security risks effectively
Any violation of IT security policy should result in immediate employee termination
explanation: Shadow IT phenomenon: common in organizations where IT governance is too centralized to be practical. Gartner research: 30–40% of corporate IT spend happens outside the official IT organization. The consequences: unknown security exposure, compliance gaps (data in unapproved locations may violate GDPR or HIPAA), and integration failures (unapproved tools don't connect to enterprise systems). The organizational lesson: extremely centralized IT governance creates shadow IT because the benefit of control is outweighed by the cost of inflexibility. Modern IT governance models: 'guardrails, not gates' — IT sets security standards and provides a pre-approved service catalog (marketing-approved cloud tools) so teams can self-serve within the guardrails, reducing the approval burden while maintaining security standards.

question: Enron Corporation (pre-collapse, 1990s) decentralized business unit authority dramatically, creating an entrepreneurial culture where unit heads had P&L autonomy and could pursue new business opportunities without corporate approval. What was the fundamental problem with Enron's decentralization design?
options: Enron's decentralization model was effective and its collapse was unrelated to organizational design

Decentralization without adequate risk controls, transparency requirements, and ethical culture: (1) Risk assessment failure — unit heads were rewarded for deal volume and reported earnings, not risk-adjusted returns; complex financial instruments created in decentralized units were not fully understood by corporate executives; (2) Mark-to-market accounting — decentralized units could 'book' future contract value immediately as current earnings (even if contracts were poorly priced), creating incentives for volume at any price; (3) Concentrated information — decentralized business units held detailed deal information that corporate couldn't fully evaluate; (4) Ethical culture failure — the competitively intense, performance-at-all-costs culture created by rank-and-yank performance management (annual firing of bottom 15%) incentivized hiding problems rather than reporting them; Enron's decentralization lacked the offsetting control mechanisms that make decentralization safe
Enron failed only due to external factors beyond management's control
Decentralization always increases financial fraud risk in companies
explanation: Enron organizational design failure: the decentralization itself wasn't wrong — P&L accountability creates entrepreneurial behavior. The failures: (1) Incentive structure: rewarded reported earnings (manipulable) rather than actual cash returns; (2) Control system: mark-to-market accounting and Special Purpose Entities (off-balance-sheet vehicles) were used to inflate reported profits — complex enough that decentralized unit managers could obscure real financial position; (3) Cultural failure: Jeff Skilling's 'survival of the fittest' culture made admitting failure career-ending → managers hid losing deals in SPEs rather than reporting them. The organizational lesson: decentralization requires proportional investment in: transparent financial reporting, risk-adjusted performance metrics, control systems that see through complexity, and an ethical culture where admitting problems is safe.

question: A restaurant chain expands internationally: Japan, France, Germany, Brazil, India, China. Should menu decisions be centralized at headquarters or decentralized to local managers? Consider McDonald's, KFC, and Starbucks examples.
options: All international menus should be identical to the domestic menu regardless of cultural preferences

Hybrid model with core-local balance: McDonald's: standardizes the core menu (Big Mac, fries, McNuggets — global brand foundations) but allows local menu additions (McSpicy Paneer in India, Teriyaki McBurger in Japan, Croque McDo in France); this achieves: consistent global brand identity (the Big Mac is the same everywhere — that's why it's used to compare currency purchasing power) while providing local relevance; KFC: significant local customization (extensive rice dishes in China, congee breakfast in Asia, fish dishes during Ramadan in Muslim markets); Starbucks: core espresso menu globally but local tea-focused beverages in China and Japan where tea culture is dominant; the principle: identify which elements create brand value through global consistency vs. which require local adaptation for customer relevance
All menus should be entirely localized with no global standards
Menu decisions must be made only by culinary experts at headquarters regardless of local preferences
explanation: Menu decentralization in fast food: the McDonald's model is the global standard example. The 'core + local' architecture: the Big Mac, fries, and Chicken McNuggets are globally consistent because they're the brand's essence (you know what McDonald's is anywhere in the world because of these items). But 'local relevance' additions (Samurai Pork Burger in Thailand, Maharaja Mac in India — the Big Mac equivalent made with chicken for Hindu customers) are critical for market acceptance. The business logic: a rigid global menu ignores fundamental dietary, cultural, and taste differences that make the food unappealing; full local menu ignores the scale advantage of global procurement and supply chain. The hybrid: global standards for core items + local freedom for additions, within brand guidelines.

question: Amazon's AWS cloud computing began as internal infrastructure that Amazon decentralized to internal development teams. This decentralized access model eventually led to Amazon externally launching AWS in 2006. How did internal organizational decentralization create an external business opportunity?
options: AWS was created by a separate company that Amazon acquired, not an internal project

Amazon's internal 'service-oriented architecture' mandate created AWS: Jeff Bezos's 2002 internal memo required all internal Amazon teams to: (1) Expose their functionality as APIs; (2) Communicate with each other only through these APIs; (3) Build these APIs as if they were being offered to external developers. This decentralized infrastructure access — each development team could access compute, storage, and database services on-demand through an API, rather than waiting for centralized IT provisioning. The internal realization: Amazon had built the world's best on-demand infrastructure platform for its own decentralized teams. Externalizing this platform (AWS) required minimal additional work — the APIs were already external-facing in design. The organizational insight: the discipline imposed by treating internal access as if it were a commercial service (documentation, SLAs, pricing) made the external launch straightforward
AWS was created specifically as an external product and never used internally
Decentralized IT access models always lead to commercial cloud platform businesses
explanation: AWS origin story: Brad Stone's 'The Everything Store' and Steve Yegge's famous internal Google memo (leaked 2011) both document the API mandate. Amazon's 2002 internal transformation: every team must offer their capabilities as APIs that other teams can call. Result: Amazon built exceptional internal tools for distributed computing, storage, and databases (the precursors to S3, EC2, and RDS). When the infrastructure team realized they had a world-class platform, the 2006 external launch was low-incremental-effort because the APIs were already designed for external consumption. The organizational lesson: internal decentralization disciplines (treating internal services as products) can accidentally create commercially valuable capabilities. This is why the organizational principle of internal API-first development continues to be widely taught as a best practice.
